reasoning: The 10-year Treasury yield recently spiked to 4.14 on Dec 5, 2025, which represents a local maximum compared to the ~4.00-4.05 range seen in late November. Because interest rates roughly follow a random walk with slight mean reversion in the short run, starting from a local ceiling depresses the immediate probability of a further strict increase. The statistical trend model points to a predicted value of 4.095 for Dec 14, implying a drop from the current 4.14 level (assigning only a 34% chance of being strictly higher). With a 1-week horizon, mean reversion back towards 4.10 is the most likely outcome, keeping the probability of an increase relatively low. As the forecast horizon extends to January and March, the initial local peak effect washes out, and increased variance drives the probabilities closer to 50%. The long-term drift remains uncertain due to mixed signals of a cooling labor market against sticky inflation under lower Fed rates, so for longer horizons, the forecast defaults closer to the base rate of a random walk.
